skills.title: Using the First Paragraph to Make Predictions
skills.note: Protects Reading Speed & Comprehension. Skim the introductory paragraph to identify the thesis statement and predict the passage theme.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Always read the first paragraph (introduction) first. It introduces the main topic and context of the passage.
  • Locate the Thesis Statement—usually the first or last sentence of the introduction—which states the main point of the entire text.
  • Use this thesis to predict the overall layout, content, and arguments that will follow in the body paragraphs.

items.q: Identify the thesis statement: 'Observations of different species of birds, both in the wild and in captivity, have shown a great deal of evidence of high levels of avian intelligence.' What is the predicted topic?
items.a: Thesis statement is the entire sentence. The predicted topic is: The passage will describe scientific experiments and behavior proving how smart birds are.

skills.title: Using the Topic Sentence to Make Predictions
skills.note: Aids Skimming. Read the first sentence of each body paragraph to find its topic sentence and predict the sub-details.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • The Topic Sentence is the first sentence of a body paragraph and expresses its main idea.
  • If the first sentence is not the topic sentence, read the second or last sentence.
  • Predict what supporting details (examples, facts, stats) will appear in the rest of the paragraph.

items.q: Identify the topic sentence: 'To combat excessive thoughts and impulses, most OCD sufferers perform certain repetitive rituals that they believe will relieve their anxiety.' Predict the paragraph's details.
items.a: Topic sentence: First sentence. Prediction: The paragraph will detail the specific repetitive rituals (checking, washing, counting) performed by OCD sufferers.

skills.title: Looking for Supporting Details
skills.note: Crucial for Detail-Matching Questions. Identify facts, statistics, examples, or quotes that back up the topic sentence.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Supporting details are the 'meat' of the paragraph—they prove the topic sentence.
  • Look for types of evidence: statistics (numbers), names, scientific facts, historical dates, or examples.

items.q: In a paragraph about Zulu beadwork being communication: 'Beads are used to make items for personal adornment, home decoration, and as a language to send status messages.' What are the supporting details?
items.a: Main idea: Zulu beadwork is a form of communication. Supporting details: 1) Items for personal adornment. 2) Home decoration. 3) Language to send status/relationship messages.

skills.title: Using Key Words
skills.note: Core Scanning Skill. Circle key nouns, verbs, dates, and names in the questions, then locate them or their synonyms in the passage.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Do not read the passage word-for-word first. Go to the questions, circle Key Words (nouns, names, dates, numbers).
  • Scan the text quickly by running your eyes over it looking for these keywords or their synonyms/paraphrases.

items.q: Question: 'On what date did Portuguese explorers arrive in KwaZulu-Natal?' What are the keywords to scan?
items.a: Keywords: 'Portuguese explorers', 'arrive', 'KwaZulu-Natal'. Scan the text for these words or dates like '1497'. Found sentence: 'Portuguese explorers gave this region when they arrived in 1497.' Answer: 1497.

skills.title: Matching Headings to Paragraphs
skills.note: Common Reading Question. Identify the core theme of each paragraph to select the correct heading from a list.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Read the paragraph's topic sentence and concluding sentence to grasp the core theme.
  • Watch out for distractors—headings that contain words from the paragraph but do not represent the main idea.

items.q: Paragraph A discusses how people think colorblindness means seeing no color, but explains it is actually difficulty distinguishing shades. Headings options: 'i. Complete Blindness', 'ii. What Is Colorblindness?', 'iii. Genetic Causes'. Select the best heading.
items.a: Analyze: Paragraph defines and corrects misconceptions about colorblindness. Compare headings: 'ii' represents the definition. Correct heading: ii. What Is Colorblindness?. 'i' is a distractor.

skills.title: Making Predictions (General Training)
skills.note: Saves Time in GT Reading. Identify text types (ads, schedules, forms) to predict where key details are placed.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Identify the text format immediately (e.g., advertisement, course descriptions, employee handbook).
  • Predict the layout: advertisements have price/location at the bottom; handbooks have bulleted policies.

items.q: You see a document with headers: 'Beginning French, Intermediate French, Advanced Spanish' along with times like '4:00-6:00 p.m.' and Mr. Sato. Predict the text type.
items.a: Examine elements: Class names, times, names of people. Identify: This is a Course Description / Class Schedule text type.

skills.title: Scanning for Specific Details (General Training)
skills.note: For Fact-Finding Questions. Look for numbers, hours, contacts, or names without reading paragraphs.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Use scanning techniques to search for exact facts in advertisements, job postings, or catalogs.
  • Ignore adjectives and side sentences; keep your eyes focused on finding punctuation, digits, or capitalized nouns.

items.q: Ad text: 'locker rooms are closed every Wednesday afternoon. Nonmembers may purchase a one-day pass.' Question: 'When can you not access the locker rooms?' Scan for the answer.
items.a: Locate keywords: 'locker rooms', 'closed'. Scan and find: 'closed every Wednesday afternoon'. Answer: Wednesday afternoon.

skills.title: Skimming for the Main Idea (General Training)
skills.note: Essential for Quick Comprehension. Skim short notices or company announcements to extract the core policy.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Read headings, subheadings, and bullet points first.
  • Skim the first and last sentence of each policy paragraph to understand company rules quickly.

items.q: A notice heading: 'Voluntary Work Policy'. Paragraph: 'Full-time employees who have been with the company for six months may take one day of paid leave per month for volunteer work.' What is the core rule?
items.a: Skim heading: Volunteer policy. Skim details: 6 months tenure required, 1 day paid leave per month. Core message: Eligible employees get paid leave to do volunteer work.

skills.title: Recognizing Synonyms and Paraphrase
skills.note: Underlies All Reading Questions. Match question keywords to synonyms used in the text to locate the correct answers.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Understand that IELTS rarely uses identical words in both questions and texts.
  • Translate nouns, verbs, and adjectives in the question into their synonyms before scanning the text.

items.q: Question: 'Who is eligible to participate?' Text: 'Adults age 18 and over only are permitted in the pool.' Match synonyms.
items.a: Compare: 'eligible' in question maps to 'permitted' in text. Match: 'Who' maps to 'Adults age 18 and over'. Answer: Adults aged 18 and older.

skills.title: Recognizing Chronological Sequence
skills.note: For Flowchart & Timeline Questions. Use sequence words and dates to track the historical order of events.
skills.details:
Strategy & Method:
  • Identify timeline markers (dates, centuries) and sequence words (*first, subsequently, thereafter, finally*).
  • Draw a mental timeline to sequence developments in historical or scientific passages.

items.q: Text: 'Before the Europeans arrived, Arab traders brought glass beads. Prior to them, Phoenician mariners brought cargoes of beads.' Sequence the traders chronologically.
items.a: Analyze timeline: 'Prior to them' means Phoenicians came before Arabs. Identify Europeans: Arrived last. Sequence: 1. Phoenicians (first) -> 2. Arab traders -> 3. Europeans (last).

passages.title: Passage 1: Avian Intelligence & Adaptability
passages.text:
Observations of different species of birds, both in the wild and in captivity, have shown a great deal of evidence of high levels of avian intelligence.Thesis statement of passage For years, scientists believed that only primates were capable of complex tool construction and problem solving. However, recent experiments with New Caledonian crows have shattered this biological assumption. These birds have been observed bending straight pieces of wire to manufacture hooksClue for Q1 (crows behavior), which they subsequently use to retrieve food from narrow chambers.

In addition to tool-making, cognitive mapping has been observed in species of migratory birds. The birds navigate thousands of miles with remarkable precision by mapping star clusters and the Earth's magnetic fieldsClue for Q2 (navigation), adjusting their trajectory based on real-time weather anomalies.

qas.q: What physical actions performed by New Caledonian crows demonstrate cognitive problem-solving?
qas.a: Manufacturing hooks by bending straight wire to retrieve food from chambers.

qas.q: What natural elements do migratory birds map to navigate accurately?

passages.title: Passage 2: The Pathophysiology of OCD
passages.text:
Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is a clinically diagnosed anxiety disorder characterized by distressing and intrusive thoughts, known as obsessions. To combat these excessive impulses, most OCD sufferers perform repetitive, time-consuming rituals (compulsions) that they believe will temporarily relieve their intense anxiety.Clue for Q1 (compulsion purpose) These rituals can include compulsive washing, meticulous checking, or repetitive counting.

Neurological research reveals that OCD is linked to communication malfunctions between the frontal lobe of the brain and the deeper basal ganglia.Clue for Q2 (brain regions) Specifically, the brain's filtering mechanism fails to dismiss non-essential warning signals, causing the patient to experience an ongoing state of emergency.

qas.q: According to the text, what is the primary purpose of compulsive rituals?
qas.a: To temporarily relieve intense anxiety caused by excessive thoughts or impulses.

qas.q: What specific brain areas have communication failures in OCD patients?
qas.a: The connection between the frontal lobe and the basal ganglia.

passages.title: Passage 3: Zulu Beadwork as a Form of Communication
passages.text:
In the Zulu nation of Southern Africa, beadwork has long served as a primary medium for social communication and cultural expression. Historically, Zulu girls hand-crafted beadwork items for themselves, their peer groups, and the young men they admired. The elaborate colors and geometric layouts of the beads communicate specific messages regarding a person's marital status, intentions, and emotional feelings.

Geometric shapes act as a structured code. For example, a single triangle with the apex pointing downward denotes an unmarried man.Clue for Q1 (unmarried man symbol) Conversely, two triangles connected apex-to-apex in an hourglass shape symbolize a married man, while married women wear beadwork featuring triangles forming a diamond shape.Clue for Q2 (married woman symbol)

qas.q: What geometric symbol indicates that a Zulu man is unmarried?
qas.a: A single triangle with the apex pointing downward.

qas.q: Who historically crafted the beadwork items in Zulu society?

vocabulary.example: Over centuries, layers of volcanic ash accumulate and form nutrient-rich subsoil.
The female bird must search for food, while the male compensates by guarding the nest.
Crows can easily differentiate between friendly humans and potential threats.
The wildfire was not started by lightning, but by human negligence near the camp.
Red and blue are the predominant colors in Zulu beadwork indicating affection.
